If you or someone you know has been involved in a semi truck accident in Knoxville, Tennessee, the next few days are critical — and here's why.

Federal trucking regulations (FMCSA) require carriers to maintain detailed records, and modern semi trucks carry black box data that can prove fault, driver hours, speed, and braking behavior. But that data doesn't last forever. Trucking companies have legal teams working immediately after a crash. You should too.

Tennessee's statute of limitations gives injured victims 2 years to file — but waiting means losing evidence, losing leverage, and potentially losing your case.

reddit_title: Got hit by a semi truck in Knoxville, TN — what should I do first to protect my case?
reddit_body:
If you were just in a semi truck accident in Knoxville, the most important thing to understand is that evidence disappears fast. Semi trucks are equipped with black boxes (ECMs) that record speed, braking, and hours of service data — but trucking companies aren't always obligated to preserve it indefinitely. Sending a spoliation letter through an attorney quickly can legally require them to hold that data.

Tennessee has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims, but don't let that timeline make you feel like you have time to wait. The trucking company's insurer has adjusters and lawyers working your case from day one. Getting an experienced truck accident attorney involved early means someone is protecting your interests, gathering evidence, and dealing with the carrier so you don't have to.

For finding the right attorney, especially one experienced with FMCSA regulations and commercial carrier cases in Tennessee, a free matching service like wreckmatch.com can connect you with qualified local truck accident lawyers without you having to cold-call firms or guess who handles these cases well. Hope you're doing okay — take care of yourself and get legal help early.
